style_brief:
STYLE BRIEF — SciSymbio article hero (editorial conceptual illustration).

Aspect ratio: 3:2 widescreen landscape.
Overall feel: editorial illustration as seen in The New Yorker, The Economist, or long-form science journalism. Serious, thoughtful, slightly melancholic. Not cartoonish. Not techno-futuristic. Not corporate. Not stock-photo.

Color palette (strict):
- Primary ink: a deep desaturated teal, approximately #0c6066
- Paper tone: warm off-white / ivory, approximately #F7F3EC, with subtle paper-grain or soft noise texture (visible but understated)
- Secondary: soft graphite charcoal #2A2E33 for line work and type-weight accents
- ONE accent only (specified per article): warm amber #D4A552 OR dusty coral #C77463 OR sage mint #9EB8A3 — used sparingly (one element, 10–15% of canvas)
- Avoid: pure black, pure white, neon, gradients that look digital, purple/magenta, saturated primaries, rainbow effects.

Rendering:
- Hand-crafted feel: slight visible linework, as if drawn with a fine ink pen and then filled with flat muted color.
- Subtle paper texture under the whole image.
- Light geometric restraint — use negative space deliberately.
- No photorealism. No 3D render look. No glossy surfaces.
- No text, no typography, no words, no labels anywhere in the image.
- No SciSymbio logo — logo is applied separately.
- No human faces shown in full; silhouettes, backs of heads, or hands only.
- No generic 'AI' clichés: no glowing brains, no circuit boards, no robot hands, no binary code, no holographic interfaces, no blue digital grids.
- No literal science clichés: no neon DNA helices, no hovering atoms, no cartoon molecules, no generic microscope imagery.

Composition:
- Single clear metaphor — resist the urge to cram multiple ideas.
- Central subject takes ~40–60% of canvas; rest is breathing room.
- Slight asymmetry is welcome; dead-center is not mandatory.
- Treat it like a book cover: it should still read at thumbnail size.

Mood: quietly intelligent. Slightly wistful. Confident restraint.

28-billion-reproducibility-tax.scene: A large, old-fashioned accountant's ledger or bound laboratory notebook, seen from slightly above, open flat on an ivory desk surface. The pages are filled with neat handwritten rows of numerical entries in the deep teal ink — but a significant portion of the entries near the center are faded, smudged, or visibly missing, as if water-damaged or never fully written. A single faint amber glow emanates from between the faded rows, hinting at something lost to time — not a literal light, more a stain of warm color soaked into the paper. In the bottom-right corner, a small fountain pen rests at an angle, its nib catching a faint amber highlight. No people. No text visible in the handwriting — it should read as 'writing' without being legible. The composition conveys: a ledger that was supposed to hold everything, but quietly leaks. Metaphor: the $28B is not lost to fraud, it is lost to what was never written down. The missing entries are the procedure.

tacit-knowledge-walking-out.scene: A view down an empty, softly lit laboratory corridor, rendered in the flat editorial illustration style. The floor is ivory, the walls deep teal. One lab coat hangs on a hook on the right wall, slightly askew, clearly just taken off for the last time — still holding the shape of the shoulders that wore it. Beneath the lab coat on the floor is a small puddle of dusty-coral shadow, as if a warm presence has left a last residue. On the left wall, a row of framed notebooks or journal spines extends into the distance, their labels illegible. The lighting is low, contemplative, end-of-day. No people visible. Perspective: the viewer is standing in the doorway, looking in. The composition conveys: the forms and the room remain; the person who knew how to use them does not. Metaphor: when a senior scientist walks out, the coat stays, the room stays, the blueprints stay — but the thing that made the experiment work is the thing that left.

video-as-evidence-alcoa-primitive.scene: A single old-style regulatory form or official document, centered on the canvas, rendered flat against ivory paper. The form has visible pre-printed fields and signature lines in the deep teal, with a small sage-mint stamp in the corner — the kind of muted green used in official archives. Overlaid on the form, occupying the right third of the document's surface, is a single graphic element: a thin rectangle with slightly rounded corners that contains an abstracted 'play button' — a small triangle — in the same teal ink, as if a film strip or video frame has been carefully pasted onto the form. The video rectangle is the same visual weight as the form's pre-printed sections, not a digital screen — it looks like it belongs on the form. Fine ink hatching surrounds the video rectangle like the border of a passport photo slot. No literal cameras. No screens with glowing pixels. The composition conveys: video is being admitted, quietly and officially, as a new kind of signature — a new letter in an old alphabet. Metaphor: ALCOA+ is a paper-era alphabet expanding to include a new letter. The illustration shows the moment the new primitive takes its seat alongside the old ones — on the same document, with the same official weight.

why-eln-cant-capture-procedure.scene: A single, slightly oversized electronic form / digital field template, rendered flat on an ivory background, shown in perspective as if tilted slightly back. The form has six or seven rectangular input fields stacked vertically, each outlined in deep teal ink with a neat label line above it (labels unreadable — they should read as 'fields' not as actual text). The fields are visibly filled in with horizontal tick marks standing in for typed entries. Between two of the middle fields, a hand-drawn amber scribble — loose, loopy, urgent, in the coral-amber color — bursts out of the rigid grid, flowing off the side of the form and trailing into the paper texture around it. The scribble is clearly not contained by any field, and clearly contains information that the form cannot hold. The scribble is the only 'messy' element in an otherwise rigid composition. No people. No devices. The composition conveys: the form captures what fits, and what does not fit is exactly what matters. Metaphor: an ELN is a schema — declared before the experiment happens. Real experiments leak outside the schema, and that leak is where the tacit knowledge lives.

onboarding-two-weeks.scene: A single hourglass, centered, rendered in flat editorial illustration on ivory. The hourglass is drawn in deep teal line-work, with sage-mint sand inside. But instead of regular granular sand, the falling sand is made of tiny abstracted shapes: small scribbles, minimal silhouettes of hands, miniature lab-glass outlines, abbreviated notes — micro-symbols of what a scientist learns during onboarding. The sand is currently mid-fall, and the column of falling material is visibly denser and faster than a normal hourglass — a wide stream rather than a thin one, as if time has been compressed. The upper bulb is still mostly full; the lower bulb has a shallow accumulated pile. A single thin curved line in the same teal, suggesting 'accumulation' or 'archive,' extends from the base of the hourglass outward into the margin, as if the captured material is building into something reusable. No clocks, no calendars, no literal books. The composition conveys: training time is not being skipped — it is being stored and reused. Metaphor: what used to take six months of one-to-one shadowing is now compressed into days, because every past experiment in the lab is already a structured training artifact that the new hire can watch and replay.
